Name: curvature_aware_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Subspace Diagnostics for Stable Quantization
Short Hypothesis: The curvature of activation subspaces, quantified via Hessian conditioning and spectral decay, provides a deterministic proxy for quantization stability, enabling precision allocation without heuristic calibration.
Related Work: Existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Precision-Allocated Sparse Attention (PASA) explore training-free precision allocation but rely on activation covariance or heuristic budgeting. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly linking Hessian conditioning and spectral decay to quantization stability, providing formal PAC-Bayes bounds and deterministic numerical stability guarantees.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant computational savings but often relies on heuristic calibration or static profiling, lacking theoretical guarantees. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on explicit spectral diagnostics of activation subspaces. By quantifying Hessian conditioning and spectral decay, CAPR identifies tokens robust to low-precision computation and enforces higher precision for ill-conditioned regions. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. CAPR computes these diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Validate the correlation between Hessian conditioning/spectral decay and quantization robustness. Use randomized SVD to compute diagnostics on hidden states and measure accuracy degradation under varying precision levels.
Experiment 2: Implement CAPR on a single-GPU setup and evaluate its throughput-accuracy trade-offs on WILDS benchmarks. Compare against static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiment 3: Test CAPR’s robustness to distribution shifts by evaluating on out-of-distribution datasets. Measure generalization stability using PAC-Bayes bounds derived from spectral diagnostics.
Experiment 4: Ablation study to isolate the contribution of curvature-aware routing versus other mixed-precision components. Quantify overhead reduction from using spectral diagnostics.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: The on-the-fly computation of spectral diagnostics may introduce latency overhead, offsetting the benefits of precision switching.
Risk 2: The assumption that curvature proxies generalize across architectures and tasks may not hold universally.
Limitation 1: CAPR is designed for single-GPU setups and may not scale efficiently to multi-GPU or distributed environments.
Limitation 2: The theoretical bounds rely on idealized assumptions about noise and perturbation, which may not fully capture real-world conditions.
